messages.content:
What part of the blood pressure negative feedback loop is the heart?
effector
receptor
control center
response
effector
why

The heart is the effector in the blood pressure negative feedback loop. An effector is the part of the negative feedback loop that carries out the response to counteract the initial stimulus. In this case, the initial stimulus is a change in blood pressure. The heart responds by increasing or decreasing its rate and force of contraction to adjust cardiac output and subsequently blood pressure. Therefore, the heart is considered the effector in this feedback loop.

What part of the blood pressure negative feedback loop is the brain?
stimulus
control center
receptor
effector
control center

What part of the blood pressure negative feedback loop is a dilated blood vessel?
effector
response
set point
receptor
effector
is arteriosclerosis is a stiffening or hardening of the artery walls

Yes, that is correct. Arteriosclerosis is a condition characterized by the stiffening or hardening of the artery walls, which can lead to high blood pressure and other cardiovascular problems. This is typically caused by the buildup of fatty deposits, calcium, and other substances in the arteries over time. The stiffness and narrowing of the arteries can restrict blood flow and increase the resistance of blood vessels, leading to high blood pressure and other complications.
is the popliteal pulse in the ankle or knee

The popliteal pulse is located behind the knee, deep in the popliteal fossa. It can be felt by applying pressure with the fingers behind the knee in the crease or hollow area that is located just above the calf muscle. The popliteal pulse is an important pulse point that can be used to assess the circulation of the lower leg and foot.
what does ABI stand for

ABI stands for Ankle-Brachial Index, which is a simple and non-invasive test used to assess the arterial circulation in the legs. The test compares the blood pressure measured at the ankle with the blood pressure measured at the arm using a blood pressure cuff and a special ultrasound device called a Doppler. The result is a ratio that indicates the level of blood flow to the legs, with lower ratios indicating reduced blood flow and increased risk of peripheral artery disease (PAD). The ABI is an important diagnostic tool for the evaluation of circulatory problems in the legs and can help to identify patients who may benefit from further testing and treatment.

What part of the blood pressure negative feedback loop is a baroreceptor?

receptor
stimulus
effector
response
receptor
where does oxygen rich blood enter the heart

Oxygen-rich blood enters the heart from the lungs through the pulmonary veins. The pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium of the heart. From the left atrium, the oxygen-rich blood flows into the left ventricle, which contracts to pump the blood out through the aortic valve, into the aorta, and to the rest of the body.
is skeletal muscle striated

Yes, skeletal muscle is striated. Striated muscle tissue is so called because the muscle fibers have a striped or banded appearance when viewed under a microscope. Skeletal muscle is made up of long, cylindrical cells called muscle fibers that are formed by the fusion of many individual cells during development. These muscle fibers have a series of cross-striations called sarcomeres that give them a striated appearance. The striations are caused by the organization of the actin and myosin filaments within the muscle fibers and are a characteristic feature of skeletal muscle tissue.
is cardiac muscle striated

Yes, cardiac muscle is striated. Like skeletal muscle, cardiac muscle is also striated, meaning it has alternating light and dark bands when viewed under a microscope. The striations in cardiac muscle cells are due to the arrangement of actin and myosin filaments, which are similar to those found in skeletal muscle. The striated appearance of cardiac muscle is an important feature that reflects the highly organized structure of the heart and helps to facilitate its rhythmic contraction. However, unlike skeletal muscle, cardiac muscle is involuntary and is under the control of the autonomic nervous system.

How do the structures of smooth and cardiac muscle differ from the structure of skeletal muscle?

smooth and cardiac musle fibers are connected by intercalated discs while skeletal muscle fibers are bound together in fascicles
smooth and skeletal muscles lack striations while cardicac muscle is striated
Smooth and cardiac muscles are striated while skeletal is not
Smooth and cardiac muscles are indivudal cells with a single nucleus

The correct statement is: 

Smooth and cardiac muscles are individual cells with a single nucleus, while skeletal muscle fibers are multinucleated cells formed by the fusion of many individual cells during development. Additionally, smooth muscle lacks striations and has a spindle-like shape, while cardiac muscle is striated and has branched cells that are connected by intercalated discs. Both smooth and cardiac muscle are involuntary muscles that function to contract and relax rhythmically, but they differ in their structure and location within the body compared to skeletal muscle.

What is the name for the natural pacemakers in the heart?

sinoatrial and atrioventricular nodes
sinoventricular and atrioseptal node
bicuspid and tricuspid node
atrial and ventricular node

The name for the natural pacemakers in the heart is the sinoatrial (SA) node. The SA node is located in the right atrium of the heart and generates electrical impulses that serve as the normal heartbeat rhythm. From the SA node, the electrical signals are spread throughout both atria to stimulate atrial contraction. The signals then pass through the atrioventricular (AV) node and to the ventricles, causing them to contract and pump blood out of the heart. The SA node is often referred to as the "natural pacemaker" of the heart because it initiates and regulates the heartbeat. The AV node can also act as a secondary pacemaker if the SA node fails, but its rate of impulses is slower than the SA node.

Which structure contains the lowest amount of oxygen?
left ventricle
aorta
pulmonary vein
vena cava

Vena cava contains the lowest amount of oxygen. The vena cava is a large vein that carries deoxygenated blood from the body's tissues to the right atrium of the heart. The blood in the vena cava contains the lowest amount of oxygen of the structures listed because it has already passed through the body and delivered its oxygen to the tissues. In contrast, the left ventricle contains oxygen-rich blood that is pumped out to the body through the aorta, while the pulmonary vein carries oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium of the heart.

Which of the following has the thickest smooth muscle wall?

arteries because the blood is under higher pressure
veins because they have to push blood against gravity
capillaries because they have to push the oxygen out to tissues

Arteries have the thickest smooth muscle wall. The smooth muscle layer of the arterial wall is thicker in comparison with veins and capillaries. This is because arteries are responsible for carrying blood away from the heart and into the body's tissues under higher pressure, and the thicker layer of smooth muscle helps to accommodate and regulate the force of blood flow. The thick smooth muscle layer in arteries also allows them to respond to changes in blood pressure and to contract or relax in response to hormonal signals or nervous system inputs. In contrast, veins have a thinner smooth muscle layer and rely more on one-way valves to prevent the backward flow of blood, while capillaries lack a smooth muscle layer altogether.
